ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Kievan Rus' is claimed as foundational heritage by Ukraine, Russia, and Belarus. Modern Russian historiography often frames it as proto-Russian, while Ukrainian historians emphasize its Kyiv-centered identity. Belarusian scholars highlight the Polatsk principality's autonomy. This database lists the entity under its Ukrainian-language name because Kyiv was the capital and primary political center. All three successor claims are documented. ETHICS-001: Ukrainian name used as primary because the capital and political center was Kyiv.

name_variants.name: Kievan Rus'
name_variants.context: Standard English historiographic name
name_variants.source: Franklin, S. & Shepard, J. The Emergence of Rus (1996)

name_variants.name: Kievskaya Rus'
name_variants.context: Russian-language name; Russian historiography claims Kievan Rus' as proto-Russian state
name_variants.source: Martin, Janet. Medieval Russia, 980-1584 (2007)

name_variants.name: Kijivska Rus'
name_variants.context: Belarusian-language name
name_variants.source: Plokhy, Serhii. The Origins of the Slavic Nations (2006)

territory_changes.description: Oleg of Novgorod seized Kyiv from Askold and Dir, uniting northern and southern Rus' under one rule
Mongol invasion under Batu Khan destroyed Kyiv and shattered the federation into fragmented principalities under Mongol suzerainty

sources.citation: Franklin, Simon & Shepard, Jonathan. The Emergence of Rus, 750-1200. Longman, 1996
Plokhy, Serhii. The Origins of the Slavic Nations: Premodern Identities in Russia, Ukraine, and Belarus. Cambridge University Press, 2006
Martin, Janet. Medieval Russia, 980-1584. Cambridge University Press, 2007

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Italian name used as primary. The Grand Duchy was created by papal decree elevating the Medici dukes. ETHICS-002: The Medici grand dukes suppressed the republican traditions of Florence and Siena. Siena was conquered by Cosimo I in 1555 and its republican government forcibly dissolved.

name_variants.name: Grand Duchy of Tuscany
name_variants.context: Standard English name
name_variants.source: Cochrane, Eric. Florence in the Forgotten Centuries (1973)

name_variants.name: Magnus Ducatus Etruriae
name_variants.context: Official Latin name used in papal bulls and diplomatic documents
name_variants.source: Diaz, Furio. Il Granducato di Toscana: I Medici (1976)

territory_changes.description: Pope Pius V elevated Cosimo I de' Medici from Duke of Florence to Grand Duke of Tuscany, formalizing Medici control over the region including conquered Siena
Popular uprising and plebiscite led to the deposition of Grand Duke Leopold II and annexation to the Kingdom of Sardinia during Italian unification

sources.citation: Cochrane, Eric. Florence in the Forgotten Centuries, 1527-1800. University of Chicago Press, 1973
Diaz, Furio. Il Granducato di Toscana: I Medici. UTET, 1976

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Italian name used as primary. ETHICS-002: The Duchy changed hands between the Visconti, Sforza, French, Spanish, and Austrian rulers. The population of Lombardy was subjected to repeated foreign domination. The Spanish period (1535-1706) and Austrian period (1706-1797) represent foreign rule imposed on an Italian population.

name_variants.name: Duchy of Milan
name_variants.context: Standard English name
name_variants.source: Bentley, Jerry. Politics and Culture in Renaissance Naples (1987)

name_variants.name: Ducatus Mediolani
name_variants.context: Latin name used in imperial diplomas
name_variants.source: Bentley, Jerry. Politics and Culture in Renaissance Naples (1987)

name_variants.name: Ducado de Milan
name_variants.context: Spanish-period name during Habsburg rule
name_variants.source: Ferroni, Giulio. Storia della letteratura italiana (1991)

territory_changes.description: Gian Galeazzo Visconti purchased the ducal title from Emperor Wenceslaus, formalizing Visconti lordship over Milan and surrounding territories
Napoleon Bonaparte conquered the duchy and dissolved it, incorporating it into the Cisalpine Republic

sources.citation: Black, Jane. Absolutism in Renaissance Milan: Plenitude of Power under the Visconti and the Sforza 1329-1535. Oxford University Press, 2009
Bentley, Jerry. Politics and Culture in Renaissance Naples. Princeton University Press, 1987

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Italian name used as primary. ETHICS-002: The Kingdom of Sardinia led Italian unification (Risorgimento), which, while celebrated as national liberation, involved the forcible annexation of southern Italian states and the suppression of southern resistance movements. The Savoy dynasty's conquest of the South led to mass brigandage and harsh military repression (1861-1865) that killed thousands of southern Italians.

name_variants.name: Kingdom of Sardinia
name_variants.context: Standard English name
name_variants.source: Smith, Denis Mack. Italy: A Modern History (1997)

name_variants.name: Regnum Sardiniae
name_variants.context: Latin diplomatic name
name_variants.source: Woolf, Stuart. A History of Italy 1700-1860 (1979)

name_variants.name: Stati Sardi
name_variants.context: Alternate Italian name for the Sardinian states on the mainland
name_variants.source: Woolf, Stuart. A History of Italy 1700-1860 (1979)

territory_changes.description: Treaty of The Hague: Duke of Savoy exchanged Sicily for Sardinia, becoming King of Sardinia while retaining Piedmont
Kingdom of Sardinia absorbed all other Italian states to form the Kingdom of Italy under Victor Emmanuel II

sources.citation: Smith, Denis Mack. Italy: A Modern History. University of Michigan Press, 1997
Woolf, Stuart. A History of Italy 1700-1860: The Social Constraints of Political Change. Routledge, 1979

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Italian name used as primary. ETHICS-002: The Kingdom of Naples was ruled by foreign dynasties for most of its existence (Angevin French, Aragonese Spanish, Habsburg Spanish, Bourbon). Local populations were subjected to feudal exploitation under absentee foreign rulers. The transition from Angevin to Aragonese rule in 1442 was accomplished by military conquest. ETHICS-003: The kingdom's end through merger into the Two Sicilies (1816) erased a distinct political identity.

name_variants.name: Kingdom of Naples
name_variants.context: Standard English name
name_variants.source: Ferroni, Giulio. Storia della letteratura italiana (1991)

name_variants.name: Regnum Neapolitanum
name_variants.context: Latin diplomatic name used in papal and imperial documents
name_variants.source: Ferroni, Giulio. Storia della letteratura italiana (1991)

name_variants.name: Reino de Napoles
name_variants.context: Spanish name during Aragonese and Habsburg rule
name_variants.source: Ferroni, Giulio. Storia della letteratura italiana (1991)

territory_changes.description: Following the Sicilian Vespers revolt, Sicily broke away from Angevin rule; the mainland kingdom became distinct as the Kingdom of Naples
The Congress of Vienna led to the formal merger of the kingdoms of Naples and Sicily into the Kingdom of the Two Sicilies under the Bourbon dynasty

sources.citation: Ferroni, Giulio. Storia della letteratura italiana: Il Cinquecento e il Seicento. Einaudi, 1991
Galasso, Giuseppe. Il Regno di Napoli: Il Mezzogiorno angioino e aragonese. UTET, 1992

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Basque name Nafarroako Erresuma used as primary because the kingdom was founded by and identified with the Basque-speaking population. The Spanish name 'Reino de Navarra' reflects the Castilian perspective. ETHICS-002: The 1512 conquest of Upper Navarre by Ferdinand of Aragon was a forcible annexation of a sovereign state. Lower Navarre retained nominal independence until absorbed by France in 1620. The Basque cultural and linguistic identity was progressively marginalized under both Spanish and French rule.

name_variants.name: Kingdom of Navarre
name_variants.context: Standard English name
name_variants.source: Collins, Roger. The Basques (1990)

name_variants.name: Reino de Navarra
name_variants.context: Castilian Spanish name
name_variants.source: Lacarra, Jose Maria. Historia politica del reino de Navarra (1972)

name_variants.name: Royaume de Navarre
name_variants.context: French name, especially for Lower Navarre under French sovereignty
name_variants.source: Lacarra, Jose Maria. Historia politica del reino de Navarra (1972)

territory_changes.description: Inigo Arista established an independent Basque kingdom in the Pyrenean region, resisting both Frankish and Umayyad authority
Ferdinand of Aragon conquered Upper Navarre, forcibly annexing the majority of the kingdom to the Crown of Castile
Louis XIII formally merged the Crown of Navarre with the Crown of France, ending the last vestige of Navarrese sovereignty

sources.citation: Collins, Roger. The Basques. Blackwell, 1990
Lacarra, Jose Maria. Historia politica del reino de Navarra, desde sus origenes hasta su incorporacion a Castilla. Caja de Ahorros de Navarra, 1972

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Catalan name used as primary because the counties were Catalan-speaking and identified as such. ETHICS-002: The Catalan Counties emerged from the Frankish Marca Hispanica, gradually gaining de facto independence. Their union with the Kingdom of Aragon in 1162 through dynastic marriage is debated: Catalan historians emphasize it as a union of equals, while some Aragonese and Spanish historians frame it as absorption. The distinct Catalan identity and institutions (Corts, Generalitat) persisted within the Crown of Aragon.

name_variants.name: Catalan Counties
name_variants.context: Standard English historiographic name
name_variants.source: Bisson, Thomas N. The Medieval Crown of Aragon (1986)

name_variants.name: Condados Catalanes
name_variants.context: Castilian Spanish name
name_variants.source: Salrach, Josep Maria. El proces de formacio nacional de Catalunya (1978)

name_variants.name: Marca Hispanica
name_variants.context: Frankish Latin name for the early period when the counties were frontier territories of the Carolingian Empire
name_variants.source: Lewis, Archibald. The Development of Southern French and Catalan Society (1965)

territory_changes.description: Frankish forces under Louis the Pious captured Barcelona from the Umayyad Emirate, establishing the Marca Hispanica as a buffer zone
Count Ramon Berenguer IV's son Alfonso inherited both the County of Barcelona and the Kingdom of Aragon, forming the Crown of Aragon while Catalonia retained its distinct laws and institutions

sources.citation: Bisson, Thomas N. The Medieval Crown of Aragon: A Short History. Oxford University Press, 1986
Salrach, Josep Maria. El proces de formacio nacional de Catalunya. Edicions 62, 1978

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Finnish name used as primary. The Swedish name Storfurstendoet Finland reflects the Swedish-speaking minority's perspective and the legacy of centuries of Swedish rule prior to Russian acquisition. ETHICS-002: Finland was transferred from Sweden to Russia by military conquest (Treaty of Fredrikshamn 1809) without consulting the Finnish population. However, the Grand Duchy enjoyed considerable autonomy within the Russian Empire, and Finnish national identity crystallized during this period. Russian Russification policies in the late 19th century suppressed Finnish autonomy.

name_variants.name: Grand Duchy of Finland
name_variants.context: Standard English name
name_variants.source: Jutikkala, Eino. A History of Finland (2003)

name_variants.name: Storfurstendoet Finland
name_variants.context: Swedish-language name; Swedish was the administrative language until Finnish gained equal status in 1863
name_variants.source: Jutikkala, Eino. A History of Finland (2003)

name_variants.name: Velikoye knyazhestvo Finlyandskoye
name_variants.context: Russian name; the Grand Duchy was part of the Russian Empire
name_variants.source: Kirby, David. A Concise History of Finland (2006)

territory_changes.description: Treaty of Fredrikshamn: Sweden ceded Finland to Russia following military defeat in the Finnish War. The Finnish population was not consulted
Finland declared independence from Russia on 6 December 1917, taking advantage of the Russian Revolution

sources.citation: Jutikkala, Eino & Pirinen, Kauko. A History of Finland. WS Bookwell, 2003
Kirby, David. A Concise History of Finland. Cambridge University Press, 2006

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Teutonic Order's state was established through the systematic military conquest and forced Christianization of the Baltic Prussian (Old Prussian), Lithuanian, and Latvian populations. The Old Prussians were effectively exterminated as a distinct people -- their language went extinct by the 17th century. This represents one of medieval Europe's most complete cultural and demographic erasures. ETHICS-001: German name used as primary because the Order operated in German. The Polish name Panstwo Zakonu Krzyzackiego reflects the perspective of Poland-Lithuania, which fought prolonged wars against the Order.

name_variants.name: Teutonic Order State
name_variants.context: Standard English name
name_variants.source: Urban, William. The Teutonic Knights: A Military History (2003)

name_variants.name: Panstwo Zakonu Krzyzackiego
name_variants.context: Polish name; Poland fought major wars against the Order including the Battle of Grunwald (1410)
name_variants.source: Christiansen, Eric. The Northern Crusades (1997)

name_variants.name: Ordensland Preussen
name_variants.context: Alternate German name emphasizing the Prussian territory
name_variants.source: Urban, William. The Teutonic Knights (2003)

territory_changes.description: The Teutonic Order began the systematic conquest and forced Christianization of Old Prussian lands, leading to the near-complete destruction of the indigenous Baltic Prussian population and culture
Grand Master Albert of Brandenburg-Ansbach secularized the Order's state, converting to Lutheranism and creating the Duchy of Prussia as a Polish fief

sources.citation: Urban, William. The Teutonic Knights: A Military History. Greenhill Books, 2003
Christiansen, Eric. The Northern Crusades. Penguin, 1997

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Livonian Confederation was established through the military conquest and forced Christianization of the indigenous Latvian and Estonian populations by German crusading orders. The native Baltic and Finnic peoples were subjected to serfdom under a German-speaking ruling class. This German Baltic feudal system persisted for centuries and created deep social stratification along ethnic lines. ETHICS-001: Latvian name used as primary because Riga was the capital and the territory corresponds largely to modern Latvia and Estonia.

name_variants.name: Livonian Confederation
name_variants.context: Standard English name
name_variants.source: Christiansen, Eric. The Northern Crusades (1997)

name_variants.name: Livlandische Konfoderation
name_variants.context: German name; the ruling class was German-speaking
name_variants.source: Christiansen, Eric. The Northern Crusades (1997)

name_variants.name: Liivimaa
name_variants.context: Estonian name for Livonia
name_variants.source: Raun, Toivo. Estonia and the Estonians (2002)

territory_changes.description: German crusading orders, particularly the Livonian Brothers of the Sword, completed the military conquest of Latvian and Estonian territories, imposing Christianity and German feudal rule on the indigenous population
The Livonian War led to the dissolution of the Confederation. Territories were partitioned between Poland-Lithuania, Sweden, and Denmark, ending the German-dominated crusader state

sources.citation: Christiansen, Eric. The Northern Crusades. Penguin, 1997
Raun, Toivo. Estonia and the Estonians. Hoover Institution Press, 2002

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Georgian-language name used as primary. ETHICS-002: The Kingdom of Georgia at its height under David IV and Queen Tamar represented a multicultural state including Armenian, Azerbaijani, and Ossetian populations. Georgian historiography celebrates this as a golden age, but the perspectives of non-Georgian populations within the kingdom deserve representation. The kingdom's fragmentation followed devastating Mongol and Timurid invasions that caused massive civilian casualties.

name_variants.name: Kingdom of Georgia
name_variants.context: Standard English name
name_variants.source: Suny, Ronald Grigor. The Making of the Georgian Nation (1994)

name_variants.name: Regnum Iberiae
name_variants.context: Medieval Latin name, using the classical designation 'Iberia' for the Caucasian kingdom
name_variants.source: Suny, Ronald Grigor. The Making of the Georgian Nation (1994)

name_variants.name: Gurjistan
name_variants.context: Persian name; Georgia had extensive diplomatic and military interactions with Persian empires
name_variants.source: Rayfield, Donald. Edge of Empires: A History of Georgia (2012)

territory_changes.region: Georgia, including Abkhazia, Kakheti, and Kartli
territory_changes.description: Bagrat III unified the various Georgian kingdoms and principalities into a single Kingdom of Georgia

The kingdom permanently fragmented into the kingdoms of Kartli, Kakheti, and Imereti, and the principalities of Samtskhe, Guria, Mingrelia, and Abkhazia, following centuries of Mongol and Timurid devastation

sources.citation: Suny, Ronald Grigor. The Making of the Georgian Nation. Indiana University Press, 1994
Rayfield, Donald. Edge of Empires: A History of Georgia. Reaktion Books, 2012

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Latin name used as primary because Merovingian administration used Latin. The Frankish language (Old Franconian) was spoken by the ruling elite but left few written records. ETHICS-002: The Merovingian conquest of Gaul involved the forcible subjugation of Gallo-Roman, Burgundian, Visigothic, and Alemannic populations. Clovis I's conversion to Christianity was both a religious and political act that facilitated the absorption of Gallo-Roman elites but also imposed Christianity on pagan Frankish populations.

name_variants.name: Merovingian Kingdom
name_variants.context: Standard English historiographic name
name_variants.source: Wood, Ian. The Merovingian Kingdoms 450-751 (1994)

name_variants.name: Royaume des Francs
name_variants.context: Modern French name
name_variants.source: Geary, Patrick. Before France and Germany (1988)

name_variants.name: Ryk der Franken
name_variants.context: Reconstructed Old Franconian name
name_variants.source: Geary, Patrick. Before France and Germany (1988)

territory_changes.description: Clovis I succeeded his father Childeric I as king of the Salian Franks, beginning the military conquest that would unite most of Gaul under Merovingian rule
Pepin the Short, with papal approval, deposed the last Merovingian king Childeric III and established the Carolingian dynasty, ending the Merovingian period

sources.citation: Wood, Ian. The Merovingian Kingdoms 450-751. Longman, 1994
Geary, Patrick. Before France and Germany: The Creation and Transformation of the Merovingian World. Oxford University Press, 1988

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Danish name used as primary because Denmark was the dominant partner. Swedish and Norwegian names (Kalmarunionen, Kalmarunionen) are identical. ETHICS-002: The union was frequently resisted by the Swedish nobility, who viewed it as Danish hegemony. The Stockholm Bloodbath of 1520, in which Christian II of Denmark executed approximately 80 Swedish nobles, was a defining act of violence that precipitated Sweden's final secession. Norwegian autonomy was progressively eroded under the union, and Norway became effectively subordinate to Denmark.

name_variants.name: Kalmar Union
name_variants.context: Standard English name
name_variants.source: Gustafsson, Harald. The Conglomerate State (1998)

name_variants.name: Kalmarunionen
name_variants.context: Swedish name; Sweden was the most reluctant member and repeatedly rebelled
name_variants.source: Gustafsson, Harald. The Conglomerate State (1998)

name_variants.name: Kalmarunionen
name_variants.context: Norwegian name; Norway's autonomy was diminished under the union
name_variants.source: Imsen, Steinar. The Union of Calmar (2003)

territory_changes.description: Queen Margaret I of Denmark united the three Scandinavian kingdoms under the Treaty of Kalmar, creating the largest state in European history by area at that time
Gustav Vasa led a successful Swedish rebellion against Danish rule, dissolving the union. The Stockholm Bloodbath of 1520 had catalyzed Swedish resistance

sources.citation: Gustafsson, Harald. The Conglomerate State: A Perspective on State Formation in Early Modern Europe. Scandinavian Journal of History, 1998
Imsen, Steinar (ed.). The Union of Calmar and After: Studies in the Scandinavian Unions. Scandia, 2003

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Croatian name used as primary. ETHICS-002: The end of the medieval Croatian kingdom in 1102 through the Pacta Conventa with Hungary is a contested historical event. Croatian historians generally view it as a personal union preserving Croatian autonomy, while some Hungarian historians frame it as a conquest or absorption. The nature of this agreement has deep implications for Croatian national identity and the historical legitimacy of Croatian statehood. Both interpretations are documented here.

name_variants.name: Kingdom of Croatia
name_variants.context: Standard English name
name_variants.source: Fine, John V.A. The Early Medieval Balkans (1991)

name_variants.name: Regnum Croatiae
name_variants.context: Latin name used in papal and royal documents
name_variants.source: Fine, John V.A. The Early Medieval Balkans (1991)

name_variants.name: Horvat Kiralysag
name_variants.context: Hungarian name; Hungary became linked to Croatia through the Pacta Conventa
name_variants.source: Goldstein, Ivo. Croatia: A History (1999)

territory_changes.description: Tomislav was recognized as the first King of Croatia, unifying the Croatian principalities and receiving recognition from Pope John X

Following the death of the last native Croatian king Petar Svacic, the Pacta Conventa established a personal union with Hungary under King Coloman. The exact terms and nature of this agreement are historically disputed

sources.citation: Fine, John V.A. The Early Medieval Balkans: A Critical Survey from the Sixth to the Late Twelfth Century. University of Michigan Press, 1991
Goldstein, Ivo. Croatia: A History. McGill-Queen's University Press, 1999

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Bulgarian name used as primary (transliterated from Cyrillic). ETHICS-002: The Second Bulgarian Empire's establishment in 1185 was a revolt against Byzantine imperial rule. Under Kaloyan and Ivan Asen II, Bulgaria became the dominant Balkan power. The empire's territory included significant Vlach, Greek, and Serbian populations. The empire's destruction by the Ottoman Turks in 1396 led to nearly five centuries of Ottoman domination that profoundly shaped Bulgarian national consciousness.

name_variants.name: Second Bulgarian Empire
name_variants.context: Standard English historiographic name
name_variants.source: Fine, John V.A. The Late Medieval Balkans (1994)

name_variants.name: Tsarstvo Balgarsko
name_variants.context: Alternate Bulgarian form
name_variants.source: Fine, John V.A. The Late Medieval Balkans (1994)

name_variants.name: Voulgarikon Vasileion
name_variants.context: Byzantine Greek name; Byzantium viewed Bulgaria as a rival and threat
name_variants.source: Curta, Florin. Southeastern Europe in the Middle Ages (2006)

territory_changes.description: The Asen brothers Peter and Asen led a successful revolt against Byzantine rule, re-establishing an independent Bulgarian state with its capital at Tarnovo
Ottoman forces under Sultan Bayezid I conquered the Bulgarian capital Nikopol and the remaining Bulgarian territories, ending the empire and beginning nearly five centuries of Ottoman rule

sources.citation: Fine, John V.A. The Late Medieval Balkans: A Critical Survey from the Late Twelfth Century to the Ottoman Conquest. University of Michigan Press, 1994
Curta, Florin. Southeastern Europe in the Middle Ages, 500-1250. Cambridge University Press, 2006

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Serbian name used as primary (transliterated from Cyrillic). ETHICS-002: Stefan Dusan's empire was built through military conquest of Byzantine territories, incorporating large Greek, Albanian, Vlach, and Bulgarian populations under Serbian rule. The empire's claim to be the successor of Byzantium is a central element of Serbian national mythology. The Battle of Kosovo (1389), which occurred after the empire's dissolution, became the foundational myth of Serbian national identity, with significant implications for modern Balkan conflicts. ETHICS-003: The use of Skopje as the capital is contested; North Macedonian scholars emphasize the region's distinct identity from Serbia.

name_variants.name: Serbian Empire
name_variants.context: Standard English name, specifically the empire of Stefan Dusan
name_variants.source: Fine, John V.A. The Late Medieval Balkans (1994)

name_variants.name: Basileia ton Serbon
name_variants.context: Greek name; Dusan styled himself Emperor of the Serbs and Greeks
name_variants.source: Fine, John V.A. The Late Medieval Balkans (1994)

name_variants.name: Imperium Serborum et Graecorum
name_variants.context: Latin form of Dusan's full imperial title
name_variants.source: Soulis, George C. The Serbs and Byzantium (1984)

territory_changes.description: Stefan Dusan proclaimed himself Emperor of the Serbs and Greeks at Skopje after conquering extensive Byzantine territories in Macedonia, Thessaly, and Epirus
Following the Battle of Maritsa, where Ottoman forces destroyed a Serbian army, the empire fragmented into competing feudal lordships that were gradually absorbed by the Ottoman Empire

sources.citation: Fine, John V.A. The Late Medieval Balkans: A Critical Survey from the Late Twelfth Century to the Ottoman Conquest. University of Michigan Press, 1994
Soulis, George C. The Serbs and Byzantium during the Reign of Tsar Stephen Dusan. Dumbarton Oaks, 1984

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Greek name used as primary. ETHICS-002: The Despotate of Morea was the last significant remnant of Byzantine civilization before Ottoman conquest. It represented a cultural renaissance in the Peloponnese, with Mystras becoming a center of late Byzantine art and philosophy (including Gemistus Pletho). The Ottoman conquest in 1460 ended the last Byzantine political entity and subjected the Greek population to Ottoman rule for nearly four centuries.

name_variants.name: Despotate of the Morea
name_variants.context: Standard English name
name_variants.source: Runciman, Steven. The Fall of Constantinople 1453 (1965)

name_variants.name: Despotatus Moreae
name_variants.context: Latin name used in Western European documents
name_variants.source: Runciman, Steven. The Fall of Constantinople 1453 (1965)

name_variants.name: Mora Despotlugu
name_variants.context: Ottoman Turkish name
name_variants.source: Nicol, Donald. The Last Centuries of Byzantium (1993)

territory_changes.description: Emperor John VI Kantakouzenos established the Despotate of the Morea, appointing his son Manuel as despot of the Peloponnese under Byzantine imperial authority
Ottoman Sultan Mehmed II conquered the Despotate, ending the last Byzantine political entity seven years after the fall of Constantinople

sources.citation: Runciman, Steven. The Fall of Constantinople 1453. Cambridge University Press, 1965
Nicol, Donald. The Last Centuries of Byzantium, 1261-1453. Cambridge University Press, 1993

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Latin Empire was established through the violent sack of Constantinople during the Fourth Crusade (1204), one of the most destructive events in medieval history. Western Crusaders, diverted from their stated goal of recapturing Jerusalem, looted and desecrated the greatest Christian city in the world. The massacre, rape, and pillage committed by Crusaders against fellow Christians was condemned by Pope Innocent III himself. Countless irreplaceable works of art, manuscripts, and relics were destroyed or stolen. This act permanently weakened Christendom's defense against Ottoman expansion. ETHICS-001: Latin name used as primary because the Crusader state operated in Latin and French.

name_variants.name: Latin Empire
name_variants.context: Standard English historiographic name
name_variants.source: Queller, Donald. The Fourth Crusade (1997)

name_variants.name: Empire latin de Constantinople
name_variants.context: French name; the ruling elite was predominantly French-speaking
name_variants.source: Queller, Donald. The Fourth Crusade (1997)

name_variants.name: Latinikon Kratos
name_variants.context: Greek name used by Byzantine historians who viewed the Latin Empire as an illegitimate usurper
name_variants.source: Harris, Jonathan. Byzantium and the Crusades (2003)

territory_changes.description: The Fourth Crusade sacked Constantinople, the greatest Christian city, establishing a Latin-ruled state on Byzantine territory. The sack involved mass murder, rape, and the destruction of irreplaceable cultural treasures
Michael VIII Palaiologos of the Empire of Nicaea recaptured Constantinople, restoring Byzantine rule and ending the Latin Empire

sources.citation: Queller, Donald & Madden, Thomas. The Fourth Crusade: The Conquest of Constantinople. University of Pennsylvania Press, 1997
Harris, Jonathan. Byzantium and the Crusades. Hambledon and London, 2003

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Greek name used as primary because the empire was a Greek-speaking successor state of Byzantium. ETHICS-002: The Empire of Trebizond was a multiethnic state including Pontic Greeks, Lazians, Armenians, and Turkic populations. Its fall in 1461 to Mehmed II led to the beginning of the gradual marginalization and eventual expulsion of the Pontic Greek population, culminating in the Greek genocide of 1914-1923 in which hundreds of thousands of Pontic Greeks were killed or expelled. The modern Turkish name Trabzon replaced the Greek Trapezous.

name_variants.name: Empire of Trebizond
name_variants.context: Standard English name
name_variants.source: Miller, William. Trebizond: The Last Greek Empire (1926)

name_variants.name: Trabzon Imparatorlugu
name_variants.context: Turkish name; the region was later absorbed into the Ottoman Empire
name_variants.source: Bryer, Anthony. The Empire of Trebizond and the Pontos (1980)

name_variants.name: Imperium Trapezuntinum
name_variants.context: Latin name used in Western European documents
name_variants.source: Miller, William. Trebizond: The Last Greek Empire (1926)

territory_changes.description: Following the Fourth Crusade's sack of Constantinople, Alexios I Komnenos established an independent Greek state on the southeastern Black Sea coast, claiming continuity with the Byzantine Empire

Sultan Mehmed II conquered Trebizond, ending the last Greek-ruled successor state of the Roman Empire. The Pontic Greek population would face centuries of marginalization culminating in the genocide of 1914-1923

sources.citation: Miller, William. Trebizond: The Last Greek Empire of the Byzantine Era. Society for Promoting Christian Knowledge, 1926
Bryer, Anthony & Winfield, David. The Byzantine Monuments and Topography of the Pontos. Dumbarton Oaks, 1985

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Gothic name used as primary, though the entity used various names over its long existence. ETHICS-002: The Crimean Goths are one of history's most enigmatic and poorly documented peoples. Their continuous existence from the 3rd to the 15th century is attested but boundaries and political structures are uncertain. They represent a remarkable survival of a Germanic-speaking population in a region dominated by Turkic, Greek, and Slavic peoples. Their absorption by the Ottoman Empire after 1475 and eventual disappearance represents a cultural extinction. ETHICS-003: Confidence score is low due to limited primary sources and significant scholarly debate about the nature and extent of Gothic political authority in Crimea.

name_variants.name: Crimean Goths
name_variants.context: Standard English historiographic name
name_variants.source: Vasiliev, Alexander. The Goths in the Crimea (1936)

name_variants.name: Principality of Theodoro
name_variants.context: Name for the late medieval Gothic principality in southwestern Crimea
name_variants.source: Vasiliev, Alexander. The Goths in the Crimea (1936)

name_variants.name: Knyazhestvo Feodoro
name_variants.context: Russian name for the principality of Theodoro
name_variants.source: Gertsen, A.G. Fortress of Doros (2003)

territory_changes.description: Gothic groups settled in Crimea as part of the broader Gothic migration period, establishing communities in the mountainous southwestern peninsula
Ottoman forces conquered the Principality of Theodoro, ending the last independent Gothic political entity. The Gothic population was gradually assimilated over the following centuries

sources.citation: Vasiliev, Alexander. The Goths in the Crimea. Medieval Academy of America, 1936
Gertsen, A.G. & Mogarichev, Yu.M. Fortress Doros: Mangup. Krymuchpedgiz, 2003

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Ukrainian name used as primary because the kingdom's territory corresponds to western Ukraine and its population spoke an East Slavic language ancestral to Ukrainian. ETHICS-002: The kingdom is claimed by both Ukrainian and Polish historiography. Ukrainian scholars view it as a direct predecessor of Ukrainian statehood, while Polish historians emphasize the subsequent Polish conquest and integration. Russian historiography has often marginalized the kingdom's distinct identity. The kingdom's fall to Poland and Lithuania in 1349 led to centuries of Polish and later Austrian rule over its former territory. ETHICS-003: Daniel of Galicia received a crown from Pope Innocent IV in 1253, making it one of the few Rus' states to be recognized as a kingdom by Western Christendom.

name_variants.name: Kingdom of Galicia-Volhynia
name_variants.context: Standard English name
name_variants.source: Plokhy, Serhii. The Gates of Europe: A History of Ukraine (2015)

name_variants.name: Halicko-Wlodzimierskie
name_variants.context: Polish name; Poland eventually conquered and absorbed the territory
name_variants.source: Plokhy, Serhii. The Origins of the Slavic Nations (2006)

name_variants.name: Regnum Galiciae et Lodomeriae
name_variants.context: Latin name used after Daniel of Galicia received a royal crown from Pope Innocent IV
name_variants.source: Dimnik, Martin. The Dynasty of Chernigov (2003)

territory_changes.description: Roman the Great united the principalities of Galicia and Volhynia into a single powerful state, creating the strongest successor state of Kievan Rus' in the west
Casimir III of Poland conquered Galicia while Lithuania absorbed Volhynia, ending the kingdom's existence and beginning centuries of Polish rule over Galician Ukrainians

sources.citation: Plokhy, Serhii. The Gates of Europe: A History of Ukraine. Basic Books, 2015
Dimnik, Martin. The Dynasty of Chernigov, 1146-1246. Cambridge University Press, 2003

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: Romanian name used as primary. ETHICS-002: Greater Romania (Romania Mare) after 1918 incorporated Transylvania, Bessarabia, Bukovina, and parts of the Banat, creating a multi-ethnic state with significant Hungarian, German, Jewish, Ukrainian, and Bulgarian minorities. The treatment of minorities, particularly the Hungarian population in Transylvania and the Jewish population throughout the country, is a deeply contested topic. Romania's territorial changes following both World Wars involved population transfers and ethnic tensions. ETHICS-003: The loss of Bessarabia and Northern Bukovina to the Soviet Union in 1940, and Northern Transylvania to Hungary, involved forced population transfers affecting millions. Romania's participation in the Holocaust resulted in the murder of between 280,000 and 380,000 Romanian and Ukrainian Jews.

name_variants.name: Kingdom of Romania
name_variants.context: Standard English name
name_variants.source: Hitchins, Keith. Romania 1866-1947 (1994)

name_variants.name: Romania Mare
name_variants.context: Greater Romania: the Romanian term for the interwar period when Romania included Transylvania, Bessarabia, and Bukovina
name_variants.source: Hitchins, Keith. Romania 1866-1947 (1994)

name_variants.name: Konigreich Rumanien
name_variants.context: German name; the Romanian royal house was the House of Hohenzollern-Sigmaringen
name_variants.source: Boia, Lucian. Romania: Borderland of Europe (2001)

territory_changes.description: The United Principalities of Romania were elevated to the Kingdom of Romania under Carol I of Hohenzollern-Sigmaringen, gaining international recognition of full sovereignty
Following World War I, Romania more than doubled its territory by incorporating Transylvania from Hungary, Bessarabia from Russia, Bukovina from Austria, and parts of the Banat
King Michael I was forced to abdicate by the Soviet-backed Communist government, ending the monarchy and establishing the Romanian People's Republic

sources.citation: Hitchins, Keith. Romania 1866-1947. Oxford University Press, 1994
Boia, Lucian. Romania: Borderland of Europe. Reaktion Books, 2001
